1.description: Motivating example. This figure shows two different distributions ofP−E$P-E$, winsorized at −1 and +2, wherePis the log hammer price (imputed at 75% of the low estimate in the case of a buy-in, where the highest bid remains below the reserve price) andEis the log mean presale estimate. The distributions are based on art auctions in 2015. We sort all lots into quartiles based on the artist-level average log return on observed resales over the period 2008 to 2014 (for initial transactions since 2003). We then compare the distribution for the first quartile (“Artists w/ low recent returns”) to the fourth quartile (“Artists w/ high recent returns”). More information on our data can be found in SectionII. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Time-series variation in sample size and prices. Panel A of this figure shows the yearly number of observations (left axis) and percentage of lots that sold successfully (right axis) in our database. Panel B shows the yearly mean and median hammer price (left axis), and mean and median ratio of hammer price to mean presale estimate (right axis) based on lots that sold successfully. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Graphical representation of our neural network. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: “Nearest neighbors” (based on image features) of selection of artworks. The first column of this matrix of artwork images shows 10 randomly chosen artworks by 10 different high-volume artists in our training data. The nine other images of each row then show the artworks that our network identifies as the first artwork's “nearest neighbors” in terms of image features. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Machine learning valuations and prices in test data. Panel A compares the distribution of machine learning predictionsMLto that of realized transaction pricesPfor our test set. Panel B shows the distribution of prediction errorsP−ML$P-ML$, winsorized at −4 and +4. Panel C shows results of a linear regression ofPonML. The line shows the predicted linear fit. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Hedonic valuations and prices in test data. Panel A compares the distribution of hedonic valuationsHRto that of realized transaction pricesPfor our test set. Panel B shows the distribution of prediction errorsP−HR$P-HR$, winsorized at −4 and +4. Panel C shows results of a linear regression ofPonHR. The line shows the predicted linear fit. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Predictions for Pablo Picasso. Panel A of this figure shows four distributions ofHRfor works by Pablo Picasso in our test set: oil paintings with a width of at least 50 cm (“large”), oil paintings with a width of less than 50 cm (“small”), nonoil artworks sold at Christie's or Sotheby's, and nonoil artworks sold elsewhere. Panels B and C show the same histograms forMLandP, respectively. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

8.description: Performance of machine learning algorithm versus hedonic model. Panel A shows the fraction of lots for whichMLis a more accurate prediction ofPthanHR(left axis), and the mean absolute prediction errors|P−ML|$|P-ML|$and|P−HR|$|P-HR|$(right axis) for deciles of lots in the test set sorted by the number of auctions of works by the artist in the training data. Panel B repeats the exercise for deciles of lots in the test set sorted by the standard deviation of log prices for the artist in the training data. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

9.description: Presale estimates and prices in test data. Panel A compares the distribution of auction house presale estimatesEto that of realized transaction pricesPfor our test set. Panel B shows the distribution of prediction errorsP−E$P-E$. Panel C shows results of a linear regression ofPonE. The line shows the predicted linear fit. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

10.description: Informational efficiency of presale estimates. The line in this figure shows the average auctioneer prediction error (i.e.,P−E$P-E$) over all lots in our test set (left axis) as a function of the orthogonalized machine learning valuationsMLorth$ML_{orth}$, which are averaged by half decile. The triangles show average buy-in rates as a function ofMLorth$ML_{orth}$(right axis). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

11.description: Drivers of performance of machine learning algorithm versus estimates. Panel A shows the fraction of lots for whichMLis a more accurate prediction ofPthanE(left axis), and the mean absolute prediction errors|P−ML|$|P-ML|$and|P−E|$|P-E|$(right axis) for deciles of lots in the test set sorted by the standard deviation of prices for the artist in the training data. Panels B, C, and D repeat the exercise for deciles of lots in the test set sorted by the predicted absolute error of machines, by the average|P−E|$|P-E|$for the artist in the training data, and by the averagePfor the artist in the training data, respectively. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

12.description: Persistence of prediction errors. Panel A shows two distributions ofP−E$P-E$, winsorized at −1 and +2, in the test set. We sort all lots into quartiles based on the artist-level averageP−E$P-E$in the training set. We then compare the distribution for the first quartile (“Artists with lowP−E$P-E$”) to the fourth quartile (“Artists with highP−E$P-E$”). Panel B repeats the exercise after classifying all lots based on the averageP−E$P-E$for each auction house-location combination. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

13.description: Predicting auctioneers' prediction errors directly. The line in this figure shows the average auctioneer prediction error (i.e.,P−E$P-E$) over all lots in our test set (left axis) as a function of the “prediction error predictions”MLP−E$ML_{P-E}$, which are averaged by half decile. The triangles show average buy-in rates as a function ofMLP−E$ML_{P-E}$(right axis). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
